title: Introduction to Programming – Loops and Control Flow Quiz

explanation.`012345`: Incorrect. The loop stops when `i` equals 5, so 5 is never printed — the `break` exits before `System.out.print(i)` is reached.
explanation.`01234`: Correct. The loop prints 0 through 4. When `i` reaches 5, the `break` statement is executed and the loop terminates immediately, so 5 and all higher numbers are never printed.
explanation.`0123456789`: Incorrect. The `break` statement at `i == 5` terminates the loop early, so numbers 5 through 9 are never printed.
explanation.`12345`: Incorrect. The loop counter `i` starts at 0 (not 1), so 0 is the first value printed. The loop also exits before printing 5, not after.

questions.source_reference: W03 In-Depth Core Concepts – Recap: Loops (For-Loop)

explanation.`1; 3; 5; 7; 9; `: Correct. Array indices start at 0, so `numbers[1]` is 1. The loop starts at index 1 and increments by 2 each iteration (`i += 2`), visiting indices 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 — which hold the values 1, 3, 5, 7, 9.
explanation.`0; 2; 4; 6; 8; `: Incorrect. This would require the loop to start at index 0 (even indices). Because the loop starts at `i = 1`, it accesses the odd-indexed elements, which hold the values 1, 3, 5, 7, 9.
explanation.`1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6; 7; 8; 9; `: Incorrect. The loop increments `i` by 2 each iteration, so every other element is skipped. To print every element from index 1 onward, the loop would need `i++` instead of `i += 2`.
Incorrect. The code compiles without errors: the `main` signature is correct, the array initialisation and for-loop syntax are both valid, and there are no type errors.

questions.source_reference: W03 In-Depth Core Concepts – Recap: Arrays and Loops (For-Loop)

explanation.`-`: Correct. The while loop increments `counter` until it equals `numbers.length`, which is 4. Since 4 does not match any explicit `case` (0, 1, 2, or 3), the `default` branch executes and prints `-`.
explanation.`zero`: Incorrect. `case 0` would only be reached if `counter` were 0 after the loop — meaning the array was empty. The array has 4 elements, so `counter` ends at 4.
explanation.`one`: Incorrect. `case 1` would only be reached if the array had exactly 1 element. Because the array has 4 elements, the loop runs 4 times.
explanation.`two or three`: Incorrect. `case 2` and `case 3` (fall-through) would only be reached if the array had 2 or 3 elements. The array has 4 elements, so `counter` ends at 4 and falls through to `default`.

questions.source_reference: W03 In-Depth Core Concepts – Recap: Loops (While-Loop) and Conditional Statements (Switch Statement)

learning_objectives: Trace the execution of a for-loop with a break statement to predict console output
Understand array indexing and the effect of a custom loop step value on which elements are accessed
Combine while-loop termination logic with switch-statement fall-through and default-case behaviour
